sentences.text: Well, Ilse was an extraordinary woman.
She was a wife of Standartenfuhrer Koch, who was one of the commandants of Buchenwald.
And she was a woman who [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was not well educated but she had gone through the public schools that were in Germany at that time and had married this man who was older than she was.
And she at that time when she was in Buchenwald was a rather attractive girl-- physically and I don't know about mentally.
But she was an eye catcher.
And she used to parade around in front of these prisoners with tight fitting clothes, and when anybody whistled or ogled, she took down their number and reported them.
And when they were reported, they were subjected to pretty severe punishment.
And that punishment consisted of being laid across a tabletop and their backsides exposed, and they were given a terrific beating-- what they call a 25.
And the prisoner had to sing out with each blow the number, beginning at one and going up to 25.
Well, a lot of these prisoners couldn't speak German, and it was impossible for them to count in German.
But a lot of them got around to learning how to count from one to 25.
And if they made a mistake in the count, they'd go back to one so that the prisoner got a lot more than 25.
As a matter of fact, as a result of these beatings many of these prisoners were-- died as a result of it.
Not only was it a shock, but it would be a shock to a man who was in good health.
But to these prisoners it was the difference between life and death.
Many of them died as a result of the beatings that they received.
And she would report these prisoners for that.
She also was interested in the lamp shades that were made out of human skin.
A prisoner would have a big Spanish galleon on his chest tattooed.

Well if Ilse saw it, she'd take down his number and he would end up down in the hospital and they'd kill him and skin him and treat the skin and make it into gloves, book ends, different articles including lamp shades.
Well, she denied all of this.
But we had a witness who was-- had sat as one of her house boys.
Her husband was a good friend of Himmler's.
And he had moved around from the different camps and was familiar with all of them.
And I think he was an inspector in some of them.
Well, while he was at Buchenwald, Koch mistreated some prisoners who were friends of Josias, Prince of Waldeck, who was the highest ranking SS man in Turania.

And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when Koch had permitted these friends of Waldeck's to be beaten and tortured and some of them died, Waldeck got the SS to investigate Koch, Ilse's husband who was a commandant at that time.
And the SS court found him guilty of not complying with the concentration camp rules.
And he was sentenced to be hanged.
Well, Himmler interceded with Hitler and that was commuted and he came back as commandant of-- continued as commandant of Buchenwald.
Well Waldeck didn't like that a bit.
And Weldeck being pretty high ranking got a hold of this Colonel Morgan who was a Standartenfuhrer in the SS, and they investigated the financial affairs of Koch while commandant at Buchenwald.
And they found that he had stolen about six million reichsmarks, and the SS court found him guilty and sentenced him to death.
And he was hanged.
And Ilse remained on living in the commandant's quarters at Buchenwald.
And she used to ride horseback and make her rounds on horseback and still exhibiting those things that the good Lord had endowed her with.
And getting ogling from the prisoners and making her reports and mistreatment.
She also took as house boys young German men and abused them as house boys.
And when she got tired of 'em, she'd send 'em down to the hospital.
And there they were beaten and liquidated, except the last one.
The last one realized what was happening.
And when Ilse went to Weimar on a shopping spree, he broke every glass in those quarters so when she came back-- and he got drunk.
And while in that drunken stupor, Ilse called up the guard house to come and fetch him.
Well, that was different from the hospital.
Ilse's friend was in the hospital.
He was one of the doctors there and he would dispose of these men when she got tired of 'em.
Well, she called up the guard house, and they sent two SS men over.
And they took this guy down to-- this prisoner down to the guard house.
They beat hell out of him, but he saved his life.
He didn't go to the hospital.
They just put him in one of the bunkers there in the guard house.
And that's how we were able to get a hold of him.
And he testified against Ilse and, and to some of her habits.
Well, I wasn't trying her for making lampshades, but that's one of the activities that occurred which was rather unusual.
97 were sentenced to be hanged.
And the balance were sentenced to life imprisonment or a period of years.
Usually long terms. 10 years [INAUDIBLE].
I really don't know.
I thought that would be a little gruesome for me to even try to investigate that.
Oh, yes.
I know and I do know that out of the 36 that were sentenced to be hanged in Dachau, there were about 31 that were actually hanged.
As a matter of fact, in Dachau and in [?
Mauthausen, ?] most of the hangings were carried out.
In Flossenburg and in Buchenwald it petered out.
There were less.
I think there were 94 that were sentenced to be hanged.
And of that 94 there were somewhere in the 60s or 70s that were actually hanged.
Well it began to change in this sense that the, the military government courts ceased to exist.
And they had brought individuals over to sit as judges in the trial of these individuals.
These were known as the subsequent proceedings.
And a lot of those cases where the death sentence was imposed, it was reduced.
And they were reduced in my cases as well, but not to the same extent.
So that there was the same sort of pattern as existed in World War I was followed.
As time passed, the heinousness of the offenses was mitigated by the passage of time.
That is correct.
I never-- I never-- I never asked a court to hang anybody.
Although I never put a person in the dock to be tried that I wasn't willing to put the noose around his neck with my own hands.
I was that convinced of his guilt.
And let me tell you, when I say convinced of his guilt-- the credibility of the witnesses was the biggest problem that we had.

Not that these events didn't occur that the witnesses testified about, but because the nature of the conduct that the witnesses described was so far out-- was so abnormal, with such departures from normal, natural human conduct-- as to be incredible.
When I first heard of it, I didn't believe it.
I thought here were witnesses that were telling me and describing to me events that occurred that couldn't happen.
Well--
Well, I can give you one that I, I recall.
In each one of these camps there was a place known as the arrest bunker.
And the arrest bunker had in it facilities for punishing the prisoners.
And one of them was known as a standing cell.
A standing cell was a device-- and there was more than one-- like a locker in a, a gymnasium.
It was big enough to stand in but you couldn't sit and you couldn't lie down.
And they would take a prisoner and put them in these standing cells and keep them there from three to five days without any food or any water or a chance for them to relieve themselves.

And it was not at all uncommon that when they would take 'em out of these standing cells, if they were still alive, they would sit-- sit them down at a table on which there had been put a vessel containing about two liters of water.
Well, the poor prisoner-- starved and almost dying of thirst-- would pick that bottle up and guzzle that water down.
That caused his stomach to expand and stick out.
Well the SS man who was in charge there would stand him up and drive his fist into that bulging stomach with such force that it usually ruptured the walls of the stomach.
And the prisoner died an agonizing death.
Well, if he couldn't stand up, they'd throw him down on the ground and stomp with their heels this bulging stomach full of water and bursting the walls of the stomach.
Now, who can imagine that one human being would do another one in that fashion?

I thought, sure, these people have probably been slapped around and mistreated in the prison-- in the concentration camps-- and that they were anxious to have retribution and that this was part of it.
But that wasn't true.
These events, while incredible to me when I first heard them, were repeated by other witnesses who had witnessed this type of activity there in the arrest bunker.
And these witness came from different parts of Europe and they didn't have a chance to get together ahead of time and make these stories up.
But the events that they described all dovetailed.
So I knew it had to happen because they were so far out-- the events were-- that two people couldn't imagine the same thing.
They had to have happened.
And I got to the point where I couldn't believe anything.
And I did believe because it did happen.
Absolutely.
Oh, of course we had some documents.
Well we had loads of documents showing the physical condition of these people.
They were bags of bones.
You could-- there was just skin over bones.
All surplus flesh had been used up.
They'd been worked to death.
That was the plan to extermination-- exterminate them by working them to death.

Now I-- I hadn't made any effort to try to collect that type of information here or present it here because those people who survived, who were actually there, are far better witnesses of what went on than I would be.
Mine was second hand.
But I didn't put anybody on the stand as a witness that I didn't feel was telling the truth at the time, and telling the truth because it was corroborated.
Oh, of course.
Of course.
That happened also.
[INAUDIBLE] Come to mind-- absolutely.
It comes to mind I had an accused that I was trying by the name of [?
Dietsch ?] in Buchenwald.
He was an assistant to Major Ding-Schuler who was carrying on experiments in Buchenwald like the doctors down in Dachau.
I'm getting so old I can hardly remember the names of all these people.
But [INAUDIBLE] for example, down in Dachau.
Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
Well, I'm just recalling the names of doctors that come to mind at this time.
These people, they testified as to what went on.
And they had no reason to lie about it.
Some of them were SS.
But to them they thought that what they were doing was right.
They had been so indoctrinated with the beliefs that were prevalent at that time in Nazi Germany that they were doing a service to their country.
And to them what they did was not a criminal offense at all.
But in spite of that, some of it was so absolutely inhuman that I don't care whether it was Nazis or who.
It had to be wrong for anybody who had a modicum of morality-- the barest of the essentials of morality.
None.
None to this extent that we tried these accused for violation of the laws and usages of war and for violations of laws that were just as valid as our criminal law, the criminal laws of Germany.
And in Nuremberg waging aggressive warfare was a new offense.
I tried them-- the accused that I prosecuted-- for violation of the Geneva and Hague Conventions.
They had been in effect since 1907 and 1929, long before there was ever a Nazi Germany.
So there was nothing ex post facto about the trials that I conducted and prosecuted there at Dachau.
What was done there in Dachau did not have the blessings of prior conduct that had been approved by humanity in general, if you please.
It was in violation of written statutes.
And those statutes were as much a part of the laws of Germany as they were a part of the laws of the United States.
That wasn't true in Nuremberg.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE]
Crimes against humanity-- that's a new one.
I didn't use crimes against humanity.
I didn't have to.
I mean all four.
No.
No.
No.
They had two rooms.
[LAUGHS] They have two courtrooms there.

And later on they had even more than that because when they started with the subsequent proceedings where they would introduce the findings of the court on the cases that I prosecuted, casting the burden of going forward with proof on those people who were then in the docks, that was not ex post facto, that was casting the burden of going forward to explain away what they did when they were there at Dachau, at [?
Mauthausen, ?] at Flossenburg, and Buchenwald.
That's correct.
Were at Dachau.
That's correct.
Well, it was kind of sparse, of course.
I was in the judge-advocate section of Headquarters Third Army.
Well, the judge-advocate section is the legal adviser to the commanding general.
My, my superior there was Colonel Cheever, who was a staff judge-advocate.
I was an assistant staff judge-advocate.
And I had charge of the administration of Military Justice in Headquarters Third Army prior to being appointed to take over the prosecution of the concentration camp cases.
My contact with General Patton, whom we had to take the court martial records down to General Patton for his approval when there were general court martials that were involved.
And I had submitted the records to General Patton.
So I had personal contact with General Patton.
Now General Truskett, he succeeded General Patton.
And I had very little contact with General Truskett because I was in the process of prosecuting these cases at Dachau.
And General Truskett was stationed down in [INAUDIBLE].
I got all the support that the law allowed.
When I was trying to get witnesses out of Vienna in for the trial of [?
Mauthausen, ?] there was a staff-judge advocate in seventh army, which was in the command of General Mark Clark.
And he didn't want to let me take the witnesses out of Vienna because he felt that the Russians would fear that we were kidnapping these people.
So I asked to speak to General Clark and I went in and spoke to General Clark and told him what I wanted to do.
And he says, well, it's not going to be a mass exodus, is it?
And I said, no, sir.
He says, we'll take them out four or five at a time and take them to Dachau, which was at Munich, and you certainly have my consent to do that.
And I did.
And we took 'em out because a lot of the witnesses in [?
Mauthausen ?] came from around Vienna and that area.
